questions: An early 2000s beginner-friendly UNIX/Linux tutorial created by a UK-based academic is distributed under an older Creative Commons license that includes Attribution, NonCommercial, and ShareAlike conditions. Identify the exact version of the Creative Commons license used for this tutorial, and provide a comprehensive summary of (1) the freedoms granted to users, (2) the specific conditions imposed on reuse, (3) any stated limitations or disclaimers, and (4) the notice comparing this version to the newer 4.0 licenses, including the canonical URL associated with the older license version.

An early-2000s beginner UNIX/Linux tutorial was released under a Creative Commons license that permits users to copy, redistribute, remix, and adapt the material, but forbids commercial use and requires derivative works to be distributed under the same terms. The license page also notes that it is an older version and recommends upgrading to a more internationally robust 4.0 version. Identify the exact name and version of this Creative Commons license (including its canonical URL format), and comprehensively describe: (1) the freedoms it grants, (2) the specific conditions imposed on users (including attribution, noncommercial, and share‑alike requirements), (3) statements about irrevocability and additional restrictions, (4) any disclaimers regarding public domain elements, warranties, or other rights, and (5) how Creative Commons describes its organizational role in providing these licenses.

rounds.question: An early 2000s beginner-friendly UNIX/Linux tutorial created by a UK-based academic is distributed under an older Creative Commons license that includes Attribution, NonCommercial, and ShareAlike conditions. Identify the exact version of the Creative Commons license used for this tutorial, and provide a comprehensive summary of (1) the freedoms granted to users, (2) the specific conditions imposed on reuse, (3) any stated limitations or disclaimers, and (4) the notice comparing this version to the newer 4.0 licenses, including the canonical URL associated with the older license version.
checklist_width: Correct identification of the license as Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0
Description of freedoms: Share (copy and redistribute) and Adapt (remix, transform, build upon)
Statement that freedoms are irrevocable provided terms are followed
Attribution requirement (credit, link to license, indicate changes)
NonCommercial restriction (no commercial use)
ShareAlike requirement (derivatives under same license)
No additional restrictions clause
Public domain/exception limitation note
No warranties disclaimer and mention of possible other rights (privacy, publicity, moral rights)
Notice that this is an older license version and recommendation to use 4.0 for new works
Statement that 4.0 licenses are more user-friendly and internationally robust
Canonical URL: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/
checklist_depth: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 + Matches tutorial’s stated older CC license with BY-NC-SA elements
Comparison to 4.0 version + Explicit notice recommending adoption of 4.0 and describing improvements
rounds.rationale: The question uses contextual clues (early 2000s UNIX/Linux beginner tutorial by a UK academic under a CC license with BY-NC-SA elements) to indirectly identify the specific license version (Deep reasoning). It then requires aggregation of multiple dispersed elements from the license deed—freedoms, conditions, disclaimers, upgrade notice, and canonical URL—ensuring broad information synthesis across the license summary (Wide scope).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Notice This is an older version of this license. Compared to previous versions, the 4.0 versions of all CC licenses are more user-friendly and more internationally robust . If
rounds.answer_a:
The tutorial uses the **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK)** license. The canonical license URL is: **https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/** [1][2].

**(1) Freedoms granted**

Users are free to:
- **Copy and redistribute** the material in any medium or format.  
- **Adapt, remix, transform, and build upon** the material.  
These freedoms are granted **worldwide** and **for the duration of copyright**, and cannot be revoked so long as the license terms are followed [1].

**(2) Conditions on reuse**

- **Attribution**: You must give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made, without implying endorsement by the original author [1].  
- **NonCommercial**: You may not use the material for commercial purposes [1].  
- **ShareAlike**: If you remix, transform, or build upon the material, you must distribute your contributions under **the same or a compatible license** [1].  
- No additional legal or technological restrictions may be applied that legally restrict others from doing what the license permits [1].

**(3) Limitations / disclaimers**

The license states that it **does not affect** moral rights, privacy/publicity rights, or other rights; and it includes a disclaimer of **warranties** and limitation of **liability** as far as permitted by law [1][3].

**(4) Notice comparing to 4.0 licenses**

Creative Commons notes that **2.0 UK** is a **port** and that the **4.0 licenses are the most up‑to‑date and internationally robust**, recommending 4.0 for new works rather than 2.0 ports [2][3].

## References

[1] Creative Commons, “Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales Deed,” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/  
[2] Creative Commons, “License Versions and Ports (overview of 2.0 vs 4.0),” https://creativecommons.org/version4/  
[3] Creative Commons Legal Code, CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK, https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/legalcode
rounds.answer_b:
The tutorial in question is the "UNIX Tutorial for Beginners" by Michael Stonebank from the University of Surrey [1]. It is distributed under the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK) license [1][2]. 

Under this older license, the (1) freedoms granted allow users to "Share" (copy, distribute, and transmit the material) and "Remix" (adapt, alter, or build upon the academic work) [3]. 

Regarding (2) conditions on reuse, users must fulfill three primary stipulations. First, *Attribution* mandates giving appropriate credit to the author, providing a link to the license, and indicating if modifications were made [3]. Second, the *NonCommercial* clause prohibits using the educational material for commercial advantage or monetary compensation [3]. Third, the *ShareAlike* requirement dictates that if users remix, transform, or build upon the material, they must distribute their resulting contributions under the exact same, or a broadly compatible, license as the original [3].

For (3) stated limitations and disclaimers, the deed explicitly clarifies that a user's fair dealing or fair use rights, alongside other statutory copyright exceptions, are in no way affected by this license [3]. Furthermore, any conditions can be waived if formal permission is obtained directly from the copyright holder, and the author's moral rights remain fully protected [3]. 

Finally, the registry page displays a (4) specific notice recommending modern alternatives: "This is an older version of this license. Compared to previous versions, the 4.0 versions of all CC licenses are more user-friendly and more internationally robust. If you are licensing your own work, we strongly recommend the use of the 4.0 license instead: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0" [3]. The canonical URL associated with this exact older license version is `https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/` [2][3].

## References
[1] Stonebank, M. (2001). *UNIX Tutorial for Beginners*. University of Surrey. Retrieved from http://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/ 
[2] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK: England & Wales License*. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/
[3] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales Deed*. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the license as Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK (England & Wales), matching the tutorial’s older BY-NC-SA 2.0 license. So both pass the core entity check.

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent A covers more checklist elements. It explicitly mentions the irrevocability of freedoms (provided terms are followed), the “no additional restrictions” clause, and includes warranty/liability disclaimers and reference to moral/privacy rights. Agent B omits the irrevocability point and does not clearly state the “no additional restrictions” clause. While B includes the fair dealing/public domain limitation note more explicitly, overall it misses more required sub-points. Both agents provide the UK port URL instead of the canonical https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/ link specified in the checklist, so neither is perfect.

Finally, Presentation & User Experience: Agent A is more structured and scannable, with clearer sectioning and bold headers. It feels more like a polished search-engine-style summary. Agent B is solid but slightly less comprehensive and structured.

Therefore, Agent A wins on completeness and formatting, though not at the MUCH_BETTER level due to minor checklist gaps (canonical URL issue).

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.question: An early-2000s beginner UNIX/Linux tutorial was released under a Creative Commons license that permits users to copy, redistribute, remix, and adapt the material, but forbids commercial use and requires derivative works to be distributed under the same terms. The license page also notes that it is an older version and recommends upgrading to a more internationally robust 4.0 version. Identify the exact name and version of this Creative Commons license (including its canonical URL format), and comprehensively describe: (1) the freedoms it grants, (2) the specific conditions imposed on users (including attribution, noncommercial, and share‑alike requirements), (3) statements about irrevocability and additional restrictions, (4) any disclaimers regarding public domain elements, warranties, or other rights, and (5) how Creative Commons describes its organizational role in providing these licenses.
checklist_width: Correct identification of the license as Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 with canonical URL https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/
Description of granted freedoms: share (copy and redistribute) and adapt (remix, transform, build upon)
Clear explanation of Attribution requirement (credit, link to license, indicate changes, no implied endorsement)
Clear explanation of NonCommercial restriction
Clear explanation of ShareAlike requirement (derivatives under same license)
Statement that freedoms are irrevocable if terms are followed
Statement prohibiting additional legal or technological restrictions
Mention of exceptions for public domain elements or applicable limitations
Disclaimer of warranties and note about other rights (privacy, publicity, moral rights)
Description of Creative Commons as a nonprofit providing free legal tools and open licenses
checklist_depth: Correct full license name and version: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0
Logic proof: Matches a license allowing sharing and adaptation, prohibiting commercial use, requiring share-alike, and identified as an older pre-4.0 version with recommendation to use 4.0
rounds.rationale: The question uses contextual clues (early-2000s UNIX tutorial, noncommercial + share-alike + attribution, older license recommending 4.0) to logically narrow the answer to CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 without naming it directly (Deep reasoning). It then requires comprehensive aggregation of permissions, conditions, disclaimers, organizational details, and URL information from the official license page (Wide scope).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Notice This is an older version of this license. Compared to previous versions, the 4.0 versions of all CC licenses are more user-friendly and more internationally robust . If
rounds.answer_a:
The license is **Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike 2.0 Generic (CC BY‑NC‑SA 2.0)**, with canonical URL: **https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/** [1].

**(1) Freedoms granted**

The license allows anyone to **copy and redistribute** the material in any medium or format and to **remix, transform, and build upon** it, for any noncommercial purpose. These freedoms apply **worldwide** and last for the full term of copyright, and they **cannot be revoked** as long as users comply with the terms [1][2].

**(2) Conditions on users**

- **Attribution**: Users must give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made, without suggesting endorsement by the licensor [1].  
- **NonCommercial**: The material may **not** be used for commercial purposes [1].  
- **ShareAlike**: If users adapt the work, they must distribute their contributions under **the same or a compatible license** (i.e., a BY‑NC‑SA license) [1].  
- Users may **not apply legal terms or technological measures** (such as DRM) that restrict others from doing anything the license permits [1][2].

**(3) Irrevocability and additional restrictions**

The license states that the licensor **cannot revoke** granted freedoms as long as users follow the terms, and that users **may not impose additional restrictions** beyond those in the license [1].

**(4) Disclaimers**

It clarifies that elements in the **public domain** or uses permitted by exceptions/limitations (e.g., fair dealing/fair use) are **not affected** by the license. It disclaims **warranties** and limits **liability** to the maximum extent permitted by law, and notes that other rights (privacy, publicity, moral rights) may limit use [1][2].

**(5) Role of Creative Commons**

Creative Commons explains that it is **not a party** to the license; it merely provides standardized legal tools and does **not offer legal services or representation** [1][2].

## References

[1] Creative Commons, “Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 Generic (Deed),” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/  
[2] Creative Commons, “Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 Generic – Legal Code,” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/legalcode  
[3] Creative Commons, “About CC Licenses and Version 4.0,” https://creativecommons.org/version4/
rounds.answer_b:
The UNIX/Linux tutorial is distributed under the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK) license, found at the canonical URL: `https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/` [1].

(1) **Freedoms Granted:** The license gives users the right to "Share" (copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format) and "Adapt" (remix, transform, and build upon the material) for noncommercial purposes [1].

(2) **Conditions:** Reusing the material is subject to three specific rules. **Attribution** requires users to provide appropriate credit, supply a link to the license, and indicate if modifications were made, without implying endorsement by the licensor [1]. **NonCommercial** strictly forbids using the material for commercial advantage or monetary compensation [1]. **ShareAlike** mandates that any remixed, transformed, or derivative versions must be distributed under the exact same license as the original [1].

(3) **Irrevocability and Restrictions:** The granted freedoms are irrevocable; the licensor cannot revoke them as long as users comply with the license terms [1]. Additionally, users are subject to a "No additional restrictions" clause, forbidding the application of legal terms or technological measures that legally restrict others from doing anything the license permits [1].

(4) **Disclaimers:** The deed notes that users are not required to comply with the license for elements in the public domain or for uses permitted by an applicable copyright exception or limitation [1]. It also disclaims all warranties, warning that the license might not provide all necessary permissions (such as publicity, privacy, or moral rights) required for certain intended uses [2].

(5) **Organizational Role:** Creative Commons explicitly states that it is not a law firm and does not provide legal advice or services [1]. Distributing, displaying, or linking to the license or deed does not establish any lawyer-client relationship between the organization and the user [1][2].

## References
[1] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales Deed*. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/
[2] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK Legal Code*. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/legalcode
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies the license as Creative Commons Attribution‑NonCommercial‑ShareAlike 2.0 Generic with the canonical URL https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/. Agent B incorrectly identifies a jurisdiction-specific variant (2.0 UK: England & Wales) and provides the /uk/ URL, which does not match the expected canonical generic license. Thus, B fails the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Agent A comprehensively covers all required checklist elements: granted freedoms (share/adapt), attribution requirements (credit, link, indicate changes, no endorsement), noncommercial restriction, share-alike requirement, irrevocability, prohibition on additional restrictions (e.g., DRM), public domain exceptions, warranty disclaimers, other rights (privacy, publicity, moral rights), and Creative Commons’ organizational role. Agent B also covers most elements, but since the underlying license is incorrect, its otherwise solid detail does not compensate for the foundational error.

Presentation & UX: Both responses are well-structured and readable, with clear sectioning and citations. However, because Agent B’s core identification is wrong, the user would be misled despite decent formatting.

Conclusion: Agent A is factually correct and comprehensive, while Agent B fails the Deep Logic requirement. Therefore, A_MUCH_BETTER.
